---
title: "Fighting for Belonging"
format: html
---

When the American Civil War began in 1861, Chinese immigrants were a small but growing presence in the United States. Many had arrived seeking economic opportunity, especially with the California Gold Rush and the expansion of global trade networks. Few could have imagined that within a few years, they would become a part of the United States’ defining conflict. Yet dozens of Chinese men enlisted in Union and Confederate forces, and their service raises an important question: What did it mean for men who were not fully accepted as American citizens to fight for the nation in military conflict? Beyond a military struggle, the Civil War was a conflict over the meaning of the nation itself–citizenship was not a guarantee, but rather created contested opportunities for negotiation. In fact, marginalized groups of people amidst the greatest heat of war were able to forge a new definition of citizenship. April 16th, 1862 saw the first national draft in American history, which was important for two reasons: 1., introduced a new, special kind of nationalism and allowance of government authority, and 2., foreign men residing in the United States sought consular help about residency and “intentions” to remain. The antebellum U.S. lacked a clear definition of national citizenship, with the states themselves determining most civil and political rights. For years following American colonization, there was an unstated, open door policy for citizenship regulation. There was no system for identification, naturalization, or even passport requirements. Burning questions of loyalty, citizenship, and race determined who could be a member and who was sent to the outskirts.
